clue: You're standing at the base of a building shaped like a stack of vinyl records. The walk ahead passes a silent-film alley, Art Deco starbursts, and a neon sign from Howard Hughes. Take a photo here — you'll want one at the start and one at the bar.
historicalFact: The Capitol Records Building at 1750 Vine St was the world's first circular office building, completed in 1956. Architect Louis Naidorf denies designing it to look like records on a turntable — but nobody believes him. The light at the top has been blinking 'HOLLYWOOD' in Morse code since opening day.

clue: Look at the sidewalk in front of 1750 Vine St. You're standing on the Hollywood Walk of Fame. Each star has a small emblem indicating the category — a camera, a TV, a record, a microphone, or a mask. Find a star with the phonograph record emblem. What shape is the star?
historicalFact: The Walk of Fame has 2,800+ five-pointed terrazzo-and-brass stars stretched across 15 blocks of Hollywood Blvd and 3 blocks of Vine St. The first batch — 1,600 stars — went in during 1960. Then the city shut it down for 8 years because the selection process was a mess.

clue: Look up at the Capitol Records Building. Count the stacked floors — each one has a wraparound sunshade that rings the building like a record on a spindle. How many floors does the tower have?
historicalFact: The 13 floors were built to the 150-foot height limit in 1950s Hollywood. The wraparound sunshades are porcelain enamel — they keep the offices cool and make every floor look like a disc on a turntable. EMI built it after acquiring Capitol Records in 1955 for their expanding stable of artists.

clue: The Walk of Fame stars use five different emblems to mark their category. Find all five by scanning the sidewalk. One looks like a movie camera. One is a television set. One is a phonograph record. One is a radio microphone. What does the fifth emblem depict?
historicalFact: The five categories are motion pictures, television, recording, radio, and live theatre/performance. A few honorees have stars in multiple categories — Gene Autry holds the record with all five. Each star costs $85,000 to create, install, and maintain.

clue: The spire on top of the Capitol Records tower has a blinking light that's been flashing a message since 1956. The message is a single word — the name of the neighborhood you're standing in. It's broadcast in a code invented in the 1830s. What word is the light spelling?
historicalFact: The light spells H-O-L-L-Y-W-O-O-D in Morse code, day and night, since April 1956. At the opening ceremony, the person who flipped the switch was Leila Morse — granddaughter of Samuel Morse, the code's inventor. You can't read Morse code by watching the light, but the fact that it's up there broadcasting to nobody in particular is the point.

clue: You've arrived at an alley that looks like nothing — a service lane between two buildings. A hundred years ago, the three greatest comedians in the world filmed here on the same block. Walk through it. Take a photo from inside the alley looking out toward the street.
historicalFact: Chaplin-Keaton-Lloyd Alley runs south of Hollywood Blvd between Cahuenga and Cosmo. Charlie Chaplin filmed The Kid here in 1921. Buster Keaton filmed Cops in 1922. Harold Lloyd filmed Safety Last! in 1923. All three films are in the Library of Congress National Film Registry. Most people walk through it every day without knowing.

clue: Find the alley that runs south of Hollywood Blvd between Cahuenga and Cosmo. Near the entrance, look for a metal plaque. It names three silent film comedians. What are the three last names on the plaque?
verificationNeeded: Confirm the Chaplin-Keaton-Lloyd Alley plaque is still in place at the Cahuenga entrance. Dedicated by Hollywood Heritage on National Silent Movie Day, 9-29-2021.
historicalFact: Hollywood Heritage dedicated the plaque on National Silent Movie Day, September 29, 2021. The alley was identified by John Bengtson, a film location researcher the New York Times called 'the great detective of silent film locations.' He matched frames from century-old films to this unremarkable service lane.

clue: Walk to 6363 Selma Ave — a quiet side street south of Hollywood Blvd. Look for a storefront with signage that sounds like it belongs in a horror film. What is the name of the museum?
historicalFact: The Museum of Death was founded in San Diego in 1995 inside the city's first mortuary, then relocated to Hollywood in 2000. The founders' stated goal: 'to make people happy to be alive.' The building was previously Westbeach Recorders, where Pink Floyd recorded. The soundproofing now dampens a different kind of noise.

clue: Walk to the Guaranty Building at 6331 Hollywood Blvd — a 12-story Beaux-Arts tower. It was built in 1923 by the same architectural duo who designed Griffith Observatory. A different organization purchased the building in 1988. Look at the ground-floor signage or branding. What organization occupies it now?
verificationNeeded: Confirm Church of Scientology signage or branding is visible on the Guaranty Building exterior at 6331 Hollywood Blvd.
historicalFact: The Guaranty Building was built in 1923 by architects John C. Austin and Frederic M. Ashley — the same pair who designed Griffith Observatory. Charlie Chaplin and Cecil B. DeMille were among its first investors. The building's loan association went bankrupt in the Depression. Scientology bought it in 1988 for $5.1 million.

clue: Two Hollywood institutions made celebrity caricatures their signature. The Vine Street Brown Derby — the most famous restaurant in Golden Age Hollywood, now demolished — covered its walls with drawings of its famous patrons. Two blocks east, a dive bar has kept the same tradition alive since 1963: a mural of 24+ caricatures in the style of a New York Times illustrator who hid his daughter's name in every drawing. The bar is still open. Walk to 6245 Hollywood Blvd. Look at the mural through the window or step inside. What name did the illustrator hide?
verificationNeeded: Confirm the Hirschfeld-style mural is visible through the Frolic Room's front window, or that 'Nina' is identifiable in the caricatures from the bar interior.
historicalFact: The Brown Derby at 1628 Vine St opened Valentine's Day 1929 and invented the Cobb Salad (Bob Cobb chopped leftovers for Sid Grauman, who'd just had dental work). Celebrity caricatures covered its walls. Clark Gable proposed to Carole Lombard in booth 54. The restaurant burned and was demolished in 1994. Two blocks east, the Frolic Room's 1963 mural carries the same tradition — Hirschfeld-style caricatures of Einstein, Gable, Monroe, Chaplin, Sinatra. One demolished, one still pouring. The drawings outlasted the Derby.
reasoning: Solver connects two physical traditions (Brown Derby caricatures, Frolic Room mural), walks to the bar, and identifies the hidden name. D5 difficulty because it chains historical knowledge of the Brown Derby (now a ghost site) with physical observation at the Frolic Room, requiring the solver to look closely at the mural and find 'Nina' — a detail that rewards careful looking.

clue: You're almost at the bar. The Pantages Theatre is right in front of you — the last movie palace built in Hollywood, dripping with Art Deco geometry. Look at the facade. Take a photo of whatever detail catches your eye first. The bar is next door.
historicalFact: The Pantages opened June 4, 1930 — vaudeville impresario Alexander Pantages' last theater and Hollywood's last movie palace. He planned a 12-story office tower above it. The 1929 stock market crash stopped construction at two stories, where it remains. For 11 consecutive years (1949-1960), the Academy Awards were held on its stage.

clue: One man owned both the theater at 6233 Hollywood Blvd and the bar next door at 6245 Hollywood Blvd from 1949 to 1954. He was an aviator, film producer, and billionaire. Look at the neon sign outside the bar — he installed it. Now look at the theater marquee. Both buildings carry his legacy. What is the bar's name, as spelled on its neon sign?
historicalFact: Howard Hughes owned both the Pantages Theatre and the Frolic Room from 1949 to 1954. He installed the neon sign that still hangs outside the bar. During those same years, the Pantages hosted 11 consecutive Academy Awards ceremonies — and the Frolic Room was where Hollywood went after the show.

clue: The Pantages was supposed to be much taller. Alexander Pantages planned a 12-story office tower above the theater. A financial catastrophe in October 1929 stopped construction. Look at the building — how many stories tall is it?
historicalFact: The 1929 stock market crash killed 10 stories of the Pantages. What was supposed to be a 12-story tower opened as a 2-story theater. The missing floors are a monument to the Depression — you can see it in the building's proportions. The facade is too elaborate for something so short.

clue: The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences had its headquarters in the Taft Building at Hollywood and Vine from 1935 to 1946. Then, starting in 1949, the Academy Awards ceremony moved to a stage one block east. Walk from the Taft Building to the theater that hosted the Oscars for 11 straight years. How far did the Oscars travel — how many blocks?
historicalFact: The Oscars moved one block — from the Taft Building (Academy HQ, 1935-1946) to the Pantages Theatre (ceremony venue, 1949-1960). The first televised Academy Awards broadcast was from the Pantages in 1953. The Frolic Room, steps from the Pantages stage door, was where nominees drank before the show and winners drank after.

clue: You're steps from your destination. Before you go in, look at the bar's facade at 6245 Hollywood Blvd. The building opened in 1930 as a speakeasy called Freddy's — patrons slipped in through a hidden door from the theater next door. A billionaire bought both buildings and added the neon sign. A woman was reportedly seen here before she became LA's most infamous unsolved murder. In 1963, an artist painted caricatures of two dozen celebrities on the interior wall. One of those celebrities won four gold medals in Berlin in 1936 while a dictator watched from the stands. His caricature is in the mural. What is his name?
verificationNeeded: Confirm Jesse Owens is depicted in the Frolic Room's Hirschfeld-style mural. The mural includes 24+ caricatures — Owens' inclusion is referenced in some sources but needs on-site verification.
historicalFact: The Frolic Room mural was painted in 1963 and restored by Oscar Ropide in 2012. The caricatures span Hollywood and beyond — Einstein, Gable, Monroe, Chaplin, the Marx Brothers, Louis Armstrong, Sinatra, and reportedly Jesse Owens. Owens won four golds at the 1936 Berlin Olympics while Hitler refused to shake his hand. He came home to a country that wouldn't let him eat at the same restaurants as his white teammates. His face on a dive-bar wall in Hollywood is an odd kind of immortality.
reasoning: Solver identifies the athlete from the biography (four golds, Berlin, 1936, dictator watching) then enters or peers into the bar to find the caricature and confirm the name. D5 difficulty because it chains persona-style identification with physical observation inside the bar — the solver must locate the specific caricature among 24+ figures. Presence required: the mural is not consistently reproduced online with all figures labeled.
